response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend a lot of time on social media every day, probably more than I should honestly, and the fake content problem drives me absolutely crazy. After losing my wife, I found myself scrolling through Facebook and Instagram more and more, especially late at night when the kids are asleep, and you would not believe the amount of complete garbage that gets shared around. It genuinely stresses me out because I worry about what kind of world my two children are growing up in when people just spread lies without thinking twice about it. The way I deal with it is pretty straightforward because I do not have patience for nonsense. First thing, I simply do not share anything unless I am absolutely certain it is real. My work as a plumber taught me to be methodical and careful, you cannot just guess where the pipes are or you will flood someone's house, and I apply the same thinking online. If something seems too outrageous or makes me angry right away, that is usually a red flag that it might be fake. I have learned to stop and actually check before reacting. What really bothers me is when I see other people, even family members, sharing obviously false health information or political nonsense. I will call it out directly, though that has caused some arguments. I do not care much about being popular or liked, but I care deeply about the truth and protecting my kids from misinformation. I have started following only sources I trust, mostly local Bologna news and a few Christian pages that seem legitimate. I also make it a point to teach my children, even though they are young, that not everything they see online is true. When I am cycling or painting, I try to disconnect completely because the constant exposure to fake content genuinely affects my anxiety. The platforms themselves should do more to stop this, but until they do, we have to be our own filters and stay disciplined about what we consume and share.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 29-year-old widowed father with high conscientiousness and high neuroticism, working as a plumber in Bologna. His lack of formal education contrasts with his parents' higher education, suggesting practical rather than academic thinking. He spends several hours daily on social media, which combined with his high neuroticism likely means fake content causes him stress. His low agreeableness suggests he would be direct and confrontational about misinformation. His high conscientiousness means he would approach the problem methodically and carefully. His Christian beliefs and role as a father of two would make him particularly concerned about moral truth and protecting his children. His low openness suggests he prefers traditional, proven methods and trusted sources rather than exploring new perspectives. The widowed status at young age adds emotional vulnerability and explains increased social media use. His practical occupation as a plumber provides concrete analogies for his approach.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, somewhat stressed, practical, protective, disciplined, and occasionally confrontational with underlying anxiety about the issue
